added.claim_text: It involved the vast majority of the world's countries—including all the great powers—forming two opposing military alliances: the Allies and the Axis.
added.subject: It

added.claim_text: World War II was the deadliest conflict in human history, resulting in 70 to 85 million fatalities, with more civilians than military personnel killed.
added.subject: World War II

added.claim_text: Tens of millions of people died due to genocides (including the Holocaust), premeditated death from starvation, massacres, and disease.
added.subject: Tens of millions

added.claim_text: Aircraft played a major role in the conflict, including in strategic bombing of population centres, the development of nuclear weapons, and the only two uses of such in war.
added.subject: Aircraft

added.claim_text: World War II is generally considered to have begun on 1 September 1939, with the invasion of Poland by Germany and subsequent declarations of war on Germany by the United Kingdom and France on the 3rd.
added.subject: World War II

qualifiers.spatial: Poland, Germany, Germany, the United Kingdom, France

added.claim_text: From late 1939 to early 1941, in a series of campaigns and treaties, Germany conquered or controlled much of continental Europe, and formed the Axis alliance with Italy and Japan, along with other countries later on.
added.subject: Germany

added.claim_text: Under the Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact of August 1939, Germany and the Soviet Union partitioned and annexed territories of their European neighbours: Poland, Finland, Romania and the Baltic states.
added.subject: Germany

qualifiers.spatial: Germany, the Soviet Union, Poland, Finland, Romania, Baltic

added.claim_text: Following the onset of campaigns in North Africa and East Africa, and the fall of France in mid-1940, the war continued primarily between the European Axis powers and the British Empire, with war in the Balkans, the aerial Battle of Britain, the Blitz, and the Battle of the Atlantic.
added.subject: the war

qualifiers.spatial: North Africa, East Africa, France, the British Empire, Balkans, Battle, Britain

added.claim_text: On 22 June 1941, Germany led the European Axis powers in an invasion of the Soviet Union, opening the Eastern Front, the largest land theatre of war in history and trapping the Axis, crucially the German Wehrmacht, in a war of attrition.
added.subject: Germany

added.claim_text: In December 1941, Japan attacked American and British territories with near-simultaneous offensives against Southeast Asia and the Central Pacific including an attack on the US fleet at Pearl Harbor.
added.subject: Japan

qualifiers.spatial: Japan, Southeast Asia, US, Pearl Harbor

added.claim_text: Following a US declaration of war against Japan, which followed one from the UK, the European Axis powers declared war on the United States in solidarity with their ally.
added.subject: the European Axis powers

qualifiers.spatial: US, Japan, UK, the United States

added.claim_text: Japan soon captured much of the Western Pacific, but its advances were halted in 1942 after losing the critical Battle of Midway; later, Germany and Italy were defeated in North Africa and at Stalingrad in the Soviet Union.
added.subject: Japan

qualifiers.spatial: Japan, Germany, Italy, North Africa, Stalingrad, the Soviet Union

added.claim_text: Key setbacks in 1943—including a series of German defeats on the Eastern Front, the Allied invasions of Sicily and the Italian mainland, and Allied offensives in the Pacific—cost the Axis its initiative and forced it into strategic retreat on all fronts.
added.subject: Key setbacks

added.claim_text: In 1944, the Western Allies invaded German-occupied France, while the Soviet Union regained its territorial losses and turned towards Germany and its allies.
added.subject: the Western Allies

added.claim_text: The war in Europe concluded with the liberation of German-occupied territories, and the invasion of Germany by the Western Allies and the Soviet Union, culminating in the fall of Berlin to Soviet troops, the suicide of Adolf Hitler and the German unconditional surrender on 8 May 1945.
added.subject: The war

qualifiers.spatial: Europe, Germany, the Soviet Union, Berlin

added.claim_text: Following the Potsdam Declaration by the Allies on 26 July 1945 and the refusal of Japan to surrender on its terms, the United States dropped the first atomic bombs on the Japanese cities of Hiroshima, on 6 August 1945, and Nagasaki, on 9 August.
added.subject: the United States

qualifiers.spatial: Japan, the United States, Hiroshima, Nagasaki

added.claim_text: Faced with an imminent invasion of the Japanese archipelago, the possibility of additional atomic bombings, and the Soviet entry into the war against Japan and its invasion of Manchuria on 9 August, Japan announced its intention to surrender on 15 August 1945, cementing total victory in Asia for the Allies.
added.subject: Japan

added.claim_text: In the wake of the war, Germany and Japan were occupied, and war crimes tribunals were conducted against German and Japanese leaders.
added.subject: Germany

added.claim_text: Despite their well documented war crimes, mainly perpetrated in Greece and Yugoslavia, Italian leaders and generals were often pardoned, thanks to diplomatic activities.
added.subject: Italian leaders

added.claim_text: World War II changed the political alignment and social structure of the globe.
added.subject: REF

added.claim_text: The United Nations (UN) was established to foster international co-operation and prevent future conflicts, and the victorious great powers—China, France, the Soviet Union, the United Kingdom, and the United States—became the permanent members of its Security Council.
added.subject: The United Nations

qualifiers.spatial: China, France, the Soviet Union, the United Kingdom, the United States

added.claim_text: The Soviet Union and the United States emerged as rival superpowers, setting the stage for the nearly half-century-long Cold War.
added.subject: The Soviet Union

qualifiers.spatial: The Soviet Union, the United States

added.claim_text: In the wake of European devastation, the influence of its great powers waned, triggering the decolonisation of Africa and Asia.
added.subject: the influence

added.claim_text: Most countries whose industries had been damaged moved towards economic recovery and expansion.
added.subject: Most countries

added.claim_text: Political integration, especially in Europe, began as an effort to forestall future hostilities, end pre-war enmities and forge a sense of common identity.
added.subject: Political integration

removed.claim_text: The Soviet Red Army's strategic offensive in eastern Romania cut off and destroyed the considerable German troops there and triggered a successful coup d'état in Romania and in Bulgaria, followed by those countries' shift to the Allied side.
removed.subject: The Soviet Red Army's strategic offensive

removed.claim_text: In September 1944, Soviet troops advanced into Yugoslavia and forced the rapid withdrawal of German Army Groups E and F in Greece, Albania and Yugoslavia to rescue them from being cut off..
removed.subject: Soviet troops

removed.claim_text: By this point, the Communist-led Partisans under Marshal Josip Broz Tito, who had led an increasingly successful guerrilla campaign against the occupation since 1941, controlled much of the territory of Yugoslavia and engaged in delaying efforts against German forces further south.
removed.subject: the Communist-led Partisans

removed.claim_text: In northern Serbia, the Soviet Red Army, with limited support from Bulgarian forces, assisted the Partisans in a joint liberation of the capital city of Belgrade on 20 October.
removed.subject: the Soviet Red Army

removed.claim_text: A few days later, the Soviets launched a massive assault against German-occupied Hungary that lasted until the fall of Budapest in February 1945.
removed.subject: the Soviets

removed.claim_text: Unlike impressive Soviet victories in the Balkans, bitter Finnish resistance to the Soviet offensive in the Karelian Isthmus denied the Soviets occupation of Finland and led to a Soviet-Finnish armistice on relatively mild conditions, [REF:
removed.subject: resistance
